data.title: Pure Storage: Quality Business, Expensive Certainty
data.description:
I’m initiating coverage on Pure Storage, Inc. (PSTG) at a time when it looks like the company is sitting at an interesting intersection. On the one hand, it is a structural winner in enterprise storage; think flash-only architecture, a recurring revenue model, exposure to AI workloads, and improving operating margins. On the other hand, it is still operating in a cyclical enterprise IT spending environment where long periods of digestion can stall a business’s momentum very quickly.

As such, the question now is whether it is a secular compounder in a cyclical industry or a cyclical hardware vendor that looks better during strong demand phases. As with most things in life, I believe it’s a mix of both, and I’m going with a Hold at this time.

First, though, I want to talk about the company itself.

Structural Moat: I See Real Edges, But Not Immunity

Pure Storage, Inc. was founded in 2009 and went public in 2015. Today, it serves more than 14,000 customers globally, including over 60% of the Fortune 500, and it employs over 6,000 people. It is a “leading provider of enterprise-grade, all-flash block, file, and object storage,” so we’re not talking about a niche flash vendor fighting for relevance. This is a multi-billion-dollar enterprise infrastructure vendor that’s competing directly with big names like Dell, NetApp, and Hewlett-Packard Enterprise (HPE) and winning more market share in all-flash arrays.

Flash-Only Architecture

I believe that its flash-only strategy is still a core differentiator. PSTG’s entire product stack is optimized around flash, and that is because it hasn’t had to defend a legacy disk portfolio. We can see that clarity in its proprietary DirectFlash modules and tight hardware-software integration. No other company controls performance, endurance, and data efficiency the way PSTG does across the stack.

I think this matters most in AI-driven workloads for a few reasons. For one, AI infrastructure isn’t forgiving when it comes to latency or throughput. Flash density and power efficiency are constraints in that environment, and the company’s product architecture is built to cater to that demand trend, especially now.

Evergreen//One And Customer Retention

Besides the technological moat, there’s Evergreen//One, its storage-as-a-service (STaaS) platform. Here’s how the company describes it:

“Evergreen//One™ is a storage-as-a-service (STaaS) platform that unifies on-premises and public-cloud storage resources into a single data storage subscription delivering a true hybrid-cloud experience.”

It allows customers to basically upgrade their controllers within the same chassis every few years, and that means they don’t have to deal with switching friction.

The convenience also means that the customers typically stick with the company for longer, and their lifetime value is much higher. So, the business now has a growing services and subscription revenue stream, and that’s part of why its annual recurring revenue (ARR) in Q3 FY26 was around $1.8 billion, a 17% increase YoY.

Competitive Positioning

PSTG competes in a highly consolidated enterprise storage market. I mentioned some of its competitors earlier, and some of them, like Dell and HPE, benefit from the added advantage of having broader server and infrastructure bundles. Then there’s NetApp, which has more entrenched enterprise relationships and hybrid cloud tooling.

PSTG’s biggest advantage in this industry is its focus. It builds single-architecture flash, a clean operating system, and has stronger customer satisfaction scores, which I can tell from its ARR and net revenue retention numbers.

The company is one of the biggest players in the all-flash array market today, and it holds around 12% of global market share, according to Porter’s Five Forces. From here, the question is about whether it can continue grabbing more market share as the market is maturing, and it has to factor in hyperscaler purchasing.

Its Growth Profile Is Strong

If you look at PSTG’s growth over the last half-decade or so, since COVID hit, you’ll see a company that has been growing very steadily. Its revenue has gone from the mid-$1B range in 2019 and 2020 to nearly $3.2 billion in FY2025, and management expects another jump to >$3.6 billion in FY2026. The business is also doing a decent job of staying profitable, albeit the stock has been very volatile over the last year.

Now, the growth looks linear on paper, but the company has had hiccups along the way. Things like enterprise pullbacks, hyperscaler pauses, and other issues have been present. Remember that this is still largely an infrastructure-spend business model that is just upping its recurring revenue via a subscription model.

That is why I think the growth is even more impressive. The sales mix is doing a lot of the heavy lifting on this front, and I believe that it will continue to change over the next few years. Subscription services are a much more profitable segment for any business because the overhead required is much less than what you need when running a hardware-focused business. So, the higher margins are coming from this segment, even when PSTG is dealing with slower product demand.

For context, the company’s gross margin has consistently stayed in the high-60s to low-70s range over the aforementioned period, but the revenue from subscriptions is what has allowed PSTG to become profitable on an operating and net income basis over the last few years. I should point out that some of those improvements have come from the hardware side too, and it’s clear that the company is using its pricing power and data efficiency very well.

Finally, we have hyperscaler exposure, which is where I think PSTG has to contend with some cyclicality. The company's topline grows quickly when there’s more demand from the hyperscalers, and the opposite is true when that demand slows down to allow them to digest the capacity. That’s why I still see the overall growth as secular, but with some cyclicality baked into the model.

Now, I’ll discuss the numbers from PSTG’s Q3 earnings report.

Financial Quality And Capital Allocation

72%+ gross margin in a hardware-involved infrastructure business is not exactly normal, but I have discussed how the software layer, data efficiency, and installed base expansion are doing the real work here.

I also touched on the ARR earlier, and at $1.8B and RPO at $2.9B, we can see how much the company’s recurring base helps with forward visibility. We can reasonably expect significant portions of those figures to reduce the binary nature of hardware orders every quarter.

The company is already profitable on a GAAP basis, but I think the non-GAAP numbers present an even more impressive picture of how it is executing today. A 20% operating margin is a clear sign that PTSG is now enjoying some benefits of scale, and that’s a very different profile from the low-double-digit margin infrastructure vendors that rely purely on volume.

In addition to that, I like that the business is structurally cash-generative and it has a clean balance sheet. PSTG ended Q3 with a bit over $1.5B in cash and marketable securities, less than $100M in debt, and it hasn’t drawn on any revolver. Being in such a liquid position gives management a lot of flexibility, and I suppose that explains why they’re actively returning capital to shareholders. On that front, they repurchased about $53M of stock in Q3 and authorized an additional $400M buyback in December. That tells me that they view the equity as investable, and that’s good news if you’re a shareholder.

You're Paying A Big Premium For Execution Here

Today, PSTG trades at around:

  • 6.3x forward sales.

  • 37x forward earnings.

  • 29x forward EV/EBITDA.

That is premium territory by every possible metric, and I think the stock is overpriced because the market now expects durability, stable margins, and ARR growth.

I’m not arguing that the business doesn’t deserve a premium, because it does, especially because its profile is much better than that of its legacy storage peers. But at 37x earnings, there’s no room for disappointment.

Let’s look at its EPS, for example. For FY26, the expectation is that the EPS will land around $1.95. FY27’s estimate is closer to $2.3. Today’s price already assumes that the business’s current trajectory will not change. But if we see slower growth, around mid-single digits, and the market decides that the stock deserves something closer to 24-26x earnings, you’re suddenly looking at a share price closer to $55-60 without any decline in earnings. That is going off the multiple compressions alone.

Also, 29x EV/EBITDA is a multiple that I’d reserve for companies that have clean, visible, and durable growth. I’m sure that PSTG will become that over the next few years, but I don’t think it has all three just yet. These multiples just scream execution risk to me, and I cannot make a case for buying into the company at this price.

Q4 And FY26 Preview: The Multiple Is On The Line

Q4 revenue should land around the $1.02–1.04B range based on prior guidance, and that’s fine. I’m more interested in the sales mix than the headline number. I want to see whether product revenue stabilized or if hyperscaler demand is still lumpy, and ARR is the bigger tell for me.

It was $1.8B last quarter, up 17%, and if ARR growth drops into low-teens territory, it would be very difficult for me to point to durability as a lever for the stock’s re-rating. I believe that at this valuation, recurring growth needs to stay well in the mid-teens, and maybe even higher.

Besides that, RPO also needs to grow faster than revenue because it underlines how much of the company’s business we can model predictably. Then, there are the margins. PSTG is doing well on the profitability front, and its non-GAAP operating margin of 20% is a solid anchor. So, if the company reports margin compression in Q4 or the word from management regarding FY27 guidance implies that we might see some margin drift, I don’t think the stock will be forgiven. At nearly 30x EV/EBITDA, I don’t see how it will get the benefit of the doubt.

Ideally, we should be looking for signs that the company can grow its revenue by double digits again while expanding its recurring base and keeping margins steady. I don’t think investors are looking for another breakout year or quarter just yet. The stock is already expensive, but stable growth may keep it ticking along nicely for a while.

I like the company, but I’m neutral on its stock at this price. I think Q4 will really test the premium, or it just might help to justify it a bit more.

data.title: Bausch + Lomb Q4 Earnings Review: Still A Buy, With Some Caveats
data.description:
Investment Overview

Bausch + Lomb Corporation (BLCO), a leading global eye health specialist and marketer and seller of ~400 products in total (source: Q3 2025 10-Q filing), announced its Q4 and full-year earnings on February 18th.

The company earned $1.4bn in revenues in Q4, up 7% on a constant currency basis, and reported a net loss of $(58m) and adjusted EBITDA of $326m. Across the whole of 2025, revenues earned amounted to $5.1bn, GAAP net loss attributed to the business was $(360m), and adjusted EBITDA came to $858m, or $891m excluding IPR&D. Revenue grew by 5% on a constant currency basis.

At the end of yesterday, shares were down <0.5%, valued at $17.7 per share, and BLCO's market cap valuation currently stands at $6.2bn.

BLCO operates three core divisions—Vision Care, which covers its consumer and contact lens products; Surgical, which includes implantables, equipment, and consumables; and Pharmaceuticals. In 2025, these three divisions earned, respectively, $2.92bn, up 6% year-on-year; $894m, up 4%; and $1.28bn, up 6%.

In terms of guidance for 2026, BLCO shares the following table in its earnings presentation:

Analysis: No Fresh Sale News, But Plenty Of Fresh Impetus

In a note shared on Seeking Alpha last year, I covered BLCO's Q4 and full-year 2024 results and provided some background on the company, its prior acquisition by Valeant Pharma—now known as Bausch Health Companies (BHC)—and its subsequent spin-out from BHC, as that company ran into difficulties.

I won't go over that same ground here; suffice it to share a statement from BLCO's Q3 2025 10-Q filing as follows:

Bausch + Lomb is a subsidiary of Bausch Health Companies Inc. (“BHC”), with BHC directly or indirectly holding 310,449,643 Bausch + Lomb common shares, which represents approximately 88% of the issued and outstanding common shares of Bausch + Lomb, as of October 22, 2025.

On August 6, 2020, BHC announced its plan to separate our eye health business into an independent publicly traded entity, separate from the remainder of BHC (the “Separation”). This resulted in the initial public offering of Bausch + Lomb (the “B+L IPO”), and our common shares began trading on the New York Stock Exchange and the Toronto Stock Exchange, in each case under the ticker symbol “BLCO”, on May 6, 2022.

Bausch + Lomb understands that BHC continues to believe that completing the Separation, which may include the transfer of all or a portion of BHC’s remaining direct or indirect equity interest in Bausch + Lomb to its shareholders (the “Distribution”), the monetization of all or a portion of BHC’s ownership interest in Bausch + Lomb, the sale of the Company (a “Sale Transaction”) or a combination thereof, makes strategic sense and that BHC continues to evaluate all relevant factors and considerations related to completing the Separation, including those factors described in BHC’s public filings.

Last February, BLCO released a statement as follows:

Taking Bausch + Lomb private with a third-party buyer was one of several options being explored to complete a full separation from Bausch Health Companies Inc. After engagement with potential buyers, that process is complete, and will not result in a transaction at this time.

While BHC may wish to sell its BLCO holding—or part of its holding—to pay down some of its debt, it ultimately did not do so last year, although Bausch Health's management did address the situation on its Q4 earnings call yesterday as follows:

So when it comes to the monetization of our B&L equity stake, really all options are on the table. I think what will guide really our monetization decisions, as we said in our prepared remarks, is really shareholder value creation.

BLCO did share a detailed overview of its ambitions for the period 2025-2028 in a presentation given at its investor day last November, however, as shown below:

BLCO's CEO Brent Saunders struck a bullish tone on the company's prospects in the near and long term on the Q4 earnings call, telling analysts:

When you hear me talk about execution today, this is exactly what I mean. In the fourth quarter, we didn't just grow. We grew smarter and faster than the market. When you see 7% constant currency revenue growth and 27% adjusted EBITDA growth, that's real operating leverage and a clear sign of our commitment to financial excellence.

As we can see in the chart above, the company appears to have driven growth almost across the board. Vision Care revenues only grew 5% year-on-year in Q4, but in Q4 2024, the division had grown in double-digit percentages. Growth drivers include contact lenses, surely a robust long-term business, and OTC dry eye products.

Long-term, not only does management expect to drive 5-7% growth to 2028, but it also believes it has an exciting contact lens pipeline that delivers ~$1.2bn in revenues, as illustrated in the slide below:

In Pharmaceuticals, the dry eye portfolio also delivered, with Miebo and Xiidra driving >$600m in revenues between them, although we may note that BLCO rival Alcon (ALC) secured approval for a new therapy, TRIPTYR, last year. With that said, this is a multi-billion dollar market, and during a presentation at the J.P. Morgan Healthcare conference in January, management noted:

We're developing the first dual action eye drop to treat dry eye combining the distinct mechanisms of the active ingredients of Miebo and Xiidra to address tier evaporation and inflammation in a single therapy. Our glaucoma pipeline asset would be the first to lower intraocular pressure and improved visual acuity, resetting the treatment for this market.

In Surgical, despite a wobble in April last year when BLCO announced a voluntary recall of several brands of intraocular lenses marketed under its enVista brand in response to reports of adverse events, described as "an increased number of reports of toxic anterior segment syndrome (TASS)" in a company announcement, the outlook remains promising, in my view, with management noting:

We anticipate the premium intraocular lenses will continue to be the primary growth driver for our Surgical business, and we believe we are well positioned to capture market share following significant portfolio expansion over the last few years

Similarly to the other two divisions, the surgical pipeline is promising—an overview of upcoming pipeline highlights was provided in the presentation as follows:

Analysis: Is BLCO Presenting Buy Signals?

Last February, I gave BLCO stock a Buy rating, noting that a >$5bn revenue per annum business that is close to breakeven deserves a far higher valuation than $5.6bn (its value at the time), and I would repeat that same thesis for 2026 and beyond also. A $5.5bn revenue business rarely trades at <$10bn market cap unless there are significant threats to the business, in my experience.

As per its 2025 10-K filing, BLCO reported cash and equivalents of $383m, total current assets of $2.98bn, current portion of long-term debt of $39m, and long-term debt and other financial liabilities of $5bn.

BLCO says (in its investor day presentation from November last year) that it is targeting a net leverage (to adjusted EBITDA) of ~3.5x by the end of 2028 and an investment-grade debt rating, defining net leverage as:

the ratio of net debt (which is calculated as total debt (its GAAP equivalent) less cash and cash equivalents) over Adjusted EBITDA (excluding Acquired IPR&D)

Personally, I feel more comfortable about a company's prospects when the GAAP and adjusted are not too far apart, and many elements of BLCO's revenue and profit forecasts are on an adjusted basis; therefore, I detect a slight red flag waving. For example, a breakdown of non-GAAP EPS adjustments taken from the earnings presentation is shown below:

With that said, with the revenues it generates, and its adjusted EBITDA margin of 23.5%, so long as BLCO keeps chipping away at its debt, and delivers the mid-to-high single digit growth it is promising, its share price and valuation ought to increase in value, to a point where the price to sales ratio climbs towards ~2x, at least, and with the projected double-digit adjusted EPS growth, perhaps profitability on a GAAP basis can be achieved also, and we all have a positive price to earnings ratio also.

As such, and despite some caveats discussed above, I am upholding my Buy rating on BLCO stock. After my last Buy call, when shares were worth ~$16, the stock sank as low as $11 (buffeted by the Trump administration's Liberation Day tariffs, I'd suspect, given this is a global business), but has subsequently recovered, and I believe we are set for more growth in 2026.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Neutral Sentiment Leaps
data.description:
Neutral sentiment among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and pessimism decreased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, decreased 4.0 percentage points to 34.5%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the first time in 12 weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 5.2 percentage points to 28.5%. Neutral sentiment is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 83rd time in 85 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 1.2 percentage points to 36.9%. Bearish sentiment is above its historical average of 31.0% for the fifth time in 12 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) decreased 2.8 percentage points to –2.4%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the second time in 12 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members how they prefer companies to return cash to shareholders.

Here is how they responded:

  • I like companies that use both dividends and buybacks: 40.8%
  • I prefer dividends for the regular income stream: 37.2%
  • I prefer stock buybacks for tax efficiency: 12.4%
  • Not sure/no opinion: 8.7%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 34.5%, down 4.0 percentage points
  • Neutral: 28.5%, up 5.2 percentage points
  • Bearish: 36.9%, down 1.2 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987. The survey and its results are available online.

If you want to become an effective manager of your own assets and achieve your financial goals, consider a risk-free 30-day Trial AAII Membership.

data.title: First Eagle Small Cap Opportunity Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Review
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the First Eagle Small Cap Opportunity Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Small Cap Opportunity Fund A Shares (without sales charge*) posted a return of 1.71% in fourth quarter 2025. Healthcare, materials and information technology were the leading contributors among equity sectors; consumer discretionary and communication services detracted and energy was flat. The Fund underperformed the Russell 2000 Value Index in the period.

Leading contributors in the First Eagle Small Cap Opportunity Fund this quarter included Hecla Mining Company (HL), Axogen, Inc. (AXGN), FormFactor, Inc. (FORM), Kaiser Aluminum Corporation (KALU) and OUTFRONT Media Inc (OUT).

Hecla Mining is the largest producer of silver in the US and Canada. The company reported better-than-expected earnings for its most recent quarter, driven by above-consensus production volume and improved efficiency. Higher commodity prices have also been a tailwind. We think the company is a high-quality operator, and we like Hecla's focus on strengthening its balance sheet.

Axogen develops products and technologies for nerve regeneration and repair. The company reported better-than-expected results for its most recent quarter. During the quarter, the Food and Drug Administration also approved a biologics license application for the company's primary product, Avance Nerve Graft. The license provides 12 years of market exclusivity in the US and paves the way for broader insurance coverage, which may increase the commercial potential of the product.

FormFactor designs and manufactures testing and measurement equipment, with an emphasis on probe cards that allow chipmakers to test products at the wafer level before they are packaged and installed in devices like cell phones. The company reported better-than-expected results for its most recent quarter as demand for FormFactor's testing products grew due to the increasing level and complexity of testing as the density and complexity of chips escalates.

Kaiser Aluminum manufactures fabricated aluminum products for aerospace and defense, beverage and food packaging, and automotive and general engineering. The company reported better-than-expected results for its most recent quarter, driven by improved pricing and sales mix in premium markets, like aerospace and packaging. We believe the company can continue to grow sales and margins as facility and product lines expansions become fully operational, serving the aerospace and coated packaging markets, respectively.

Outfront Media is an advertising company that has billboards and transit displays. The company has been converting traditional static billboard advertising to digital displays, which has brought in new advertisers and driven growth. One of the company's key contracts is with the New York Metropolitan Transit Authority (MTA). The digital displays have been generating more revenue per display because multiple advertisements are able to run in rotation with dynamic content updates, as well as increasing ridership in the MTA.

The leading detractors in the quarter were Treace Medical Concepts, Inc. (TMCI), Thryv Holdings, Inc. (THRY), Backblaze, Inc. Class A (BLZE), Janus International Group, Inc. (JBI) and Universal Technical Institute, Inc (UTI).

Treace Medical is an orthopedic medical device company focused on bunion and midfoot surgery. Shares have been pressured by share loss to less invasive procedures. With a large addressable market and Treace's own move toward less invasive systems, our investment thesis remains intact. The company is improving its operating efficiency and has a strong balance sheet.

Thryv provides a software platform for customer relationship management and digital and print marketing services for small businesses. Weakness during the quarter reflected challenges upselling legacy customers to its software services. Because Thryv remains committed to cross-selling to higher-margin services, it is applying ample cash flows from legacy activities to strengthen its balance sheet.

Backblaze provides cloud storage and computer backup solutions. After a strong first half in 2025, decelerating growth trends in cloud storage among their target customer base of AI-related startups emerged alongside changed corporate accounting practices. Given the lack of clarity for the company going forward, we exited our position.

Janus is a global manufacturer and supplier of solutions for the self-storage industry, providing doors, hallway systems, mobile storage units, automation systems and smart-entry technologies. Revenue and earnings fell short for the quarter, because some smaller clients paused orders for repair and maintenance work, but management is confident these delayed projects will ultimately resume. Janus continues to enjoy strong market share, resilient cash flow and growth potential in smart technologies.

Universal Technical Institute offers transportation and skilled-trades training programs, as well as continuing education programs in the allied health, dental, nursing, patient care and diagnostic fields. Shares traded down as expansion costs from new campuses and educational products pressured near-term profitability. UTI is positioned to benefit from anticipated ongoing strong demand for skilled labor.

We appreciate your confidence and thank you for your support.

data.title: First Eagle Small Cap Opportunity Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The good news is that 2025 was the third consecutive year of gains by small cap stocks. The bad news is that it was the fifth consecutive year that small caps lagged larger names.

The Russell 2000 Index's 2.2% advance in the fourth quarter contributed to an annual total return of 12.8%; this compares to respective gains of 2.7% and 17.9% by the S&P 500 Index. That said, we are encouraged by the momentum evident in the small cap market. Notably, the Russell 2000 has rallied more than 42% since hitting its 2025 low in early April, outpacing the S&P 500 by nearly 375 basis points. 1

Have the Winds Begun to Shift in Favor of Small Caps?

Smaller company stocks in general have appeared cheap for some time, in our estimation, and history has taught us that they can stay this way for an extended period before the market recognizes their value. As fundamental investors, we believe that earnings growth is among the more durable catalysts for such recognition and can help boost performance even without multiple expansion. There are signs that a resurgence in small cap earnings may at last be underway after a long period in the shadow of the market's larger names.

Amid a backdrop of 4.3% GDP growth and manageable inflation, Russell 2000 year-over-year growth in the third quarter came in at nearly 64%, trouncing the consensus estimate heading into reporting season. Forecasts call for a similar level of growth in the fourth quarter, bringing calendar 2025 earnings growth to about 45%—well above the S&P 500's 13% growth. This trend is expected to persist through periods for which estimates have been published, with the Russell 2000 forecast to deliver earnings growth of 63% in 2026 and 32% in 2027 compared to 16% and 15%, respectively, for the S&P 500. Similarly, the small cap revenue growth rate is forecast to improve over the next several quarters and ultimately surpass that of large cap stocks. 2

Helping support improved small company earnings has been the lower cost—and greater availability—of capital. Smaller companies tend to be far more reliant on short-term, floating-rate debt compared to large cap names. Since the Federal Reserve's rate-cut cycle began in

September 2024, the secured overnight financing rate (SOFR)—the reference rate for many smaller borrowers' loans—declined more than 160 basis points, providing companies carrying variable-rate debt with hard-dollar savings. Because SOFR trades very closely with the federal funds rate, additional rate cuts in 2026—futures markets suggest the fed funds rate will be 25 basis points lower at midyear and 50 basis points lower by year end—could provide small companies with further savings on interest expenses. 3

More stable interest rates could also help support the market for initial public offerings (IPOs), which has been slowly coming back to life after cratering in 2022 with the onset of Fed rate hikes. Even amid 2025's tariff volatility and government shutdown, 202 IPOs were priced during the year, a four-year high and 35% increase from 2024. 4 Given an administration that has demonstrated a hands-off approach to regulatory matters, continued improvement in IPO activity during 2026 would not come as a surprise, especially as financial buyers look to monetize the investments they have held for several years.

A Tentative First Step

The strong rally in smaller stocks following the post-Liberation Day swoon reminded us that this beast still has claws. 5 Despite this recent show of strength, longer-term performance trends remain skewed toward large names, with the relative performance of small caps remaining near previous cyclical troughs. 6

Respecting the tendency for reversion to the mean, our confidence in eventual strong sustained returns from small caps—driven by fundamentals—remains firm. In the meantime, we'll remain focused on ensuring our portfolio is cheaper than the market and well-positioned for upside should recent momentum persist.

  1. 1. Source: FactSet; data as of December 31, 2025.
  2. 2. Source: LSEG I/B/E/S; data as of January 9, 2026.
  3. 3. Source: CME FedWatch; data as of January 12, 2026.
  4. 4. Source: Renaissance Capital; data as of January 2, 2026
  5. 5. Source: FactSet; data as of December 31, 2025.

Portfolio Review

Small Cap Opportunity Fund A Shares (without sales charge*) posted a return of 1.71% in fourth quarter 2025. Healthcare, materials and information technology were the leading contributors among equity sectors; consumer discretionary and communication services detracted and energy was flat. The Fund underperformed the Russell 2000 Value Index in the period.

Leading contributors in the First Eagle Small Cap Opportunity Fund this quarter included Hecla Mining Company (HL), Axogen, Inc. (AXGN), FormFactor, Inc. (FORM), Kaiser Aluminum Corporation (KALU) and OUTFRONT Media Inc (OUT).

Hecla Mining is the largest producer of silver in the US and Canada. The company reported better-than-expected earnings for its most recent quarter, driven by above-consensus production volume and improved efficiency. Higher commodity prices have also been a tailwind. We think the company is a high-quality operator, and we like Hecla's focus on strengthening its balance sheet.

Axogen develops products and technologies for nerve regeneration and repair. The company reported better-than-expected results for its most recent quarter. During the quarter, the Food and Drug Administration also approved a biologics license application for the company's primary product, Avance Nerve Graft. The license provides 12 years of market exclusivity in the US and paves the way for broader insurance coverage, which may increase the commercial potential of the product.

FormFactor designs and manufactures testing and measurement equipment, with an emphasis on probe cards that allow chipmakers to test products at the wafer level before they are packaged and installed in devices like cell phones. The company reported better-than-expected results for its most recent quarter as demand for FormFactor's testing products grew due to the increasing level and complexity of testing as the density and complexity of chips escalates.

Kaiser Aluminum manufactures fabricated aluminum products for aerospace and defense, beverage and food packaging, and automotive and general engineering. The company reported better-than-expected results for its most recent quarter, driven by improved pricing and sales mix in premium markets, like aerospace and packaging. We believe the company can continue to grow sales and margins as facility and product lines expansions become fully operational, serving the aerospace and coated packaging markets, respectively.

Outfront Media is an advertising company that has billboards and transit displays. The company has been converting traditional static billboard advertising to digital displays, which has brought in new advertisers and driven growth. One of the company's key contracts is with the New York Metropolitan Transit Authority (MTA). The digital displays have been generating more revenue per display because multiple advertisements are able to run in rotation with dynamic content updates, as well as increasing ridership in the MTA.

The leading detractors in the quarter were Treace Medical Concepts, Inc. (TMCI), Thryv Holdings, Inc. (THRY), Backblaze, Inc. Class A (BLZE), Janus International Group, Inc. (JBI) and Universal Technical Institute, Inc (UTI).

Treace Medical is an orthopedic medical device company focused on bunion and midfoot surgery. Shares have been pressured by share loss to less invasive procedures. With a large addressable market and Treace's own move toward less invasive systems, our investment thesis remains intact. The company is improving its operating efficiency and has a strong balance sheet.

Thryv provides a software platform for customer relationship management and digital and print marketing services for small businesses. Weakness during the quarter reflected challenges upselling legacy customers to its software services. Because Thryv remains committed to cross-selling to higher-margin services, it is applying ample cash flows from legacy activities to strengthen its balance sheet.

Backblaze provides cloud storage and computer backup solutions. After a strong first half in 2025, decelerating growth trends in cloud storage among their target customer base of AI-related startups emerged alongside changed corporate accounting practices. Given the lack of clarity for the company going forward, we exited our position.

Janus is a global manufacturer and supplier of solutions for the self-storage industry, providing doors, hallway systems, mobile storage units, automation systems and smart-entry technologies. Revenue and earnings fell short for the quarter, because some smaller clients paused orders for repair and maintenance work, but management is confident these delayed projects will ultimately resume. Janus continues to enjoy strong market share, resilient cash flow and growth potential in smart technologies.

Universal Technical Institute offers transportation and skilled-trades training programs, as well as continuing education programs in the allied health, dental, nursing, patient care and diagnostic fields. Shares traded down as expansion costs from new campuses and educational products pressured near-term profitability. UTI is positioned to benefit from anticipated ongoing strong demand for skilled labor.

We appreciate your confidence and thank you for your support.

Sincerely,

First Eagle Investments

data.title: PayPal: Losing Share, But EPS Could Grow Almost Double Digit In 2026
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Artisan Select Equity Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

We promised some commentary on PayPal (PYPL), one of our worst decisions in the past couple of years. Though it doesn't register on the list of Q4 top three worst contributors, it was our worst performing stock for the full year, down 31%.

PayPal is one of the largest two-sided payment networks in the world and earns attractive economics when consumers pay with the branded PayPal "button" online. The company had been poorly managed in the past, not investing enough in the branded checkout experience. This caused the business to lose share to Apple Pay and Shopify's ShopPay and to undergrow e-commerce spending. A new management team took over in 2023 and instituted a series of changes designed to boost branded checkout growth and also to improve the profitability of its lower margin business. We invested as we learned more about this strategy and saw its theoretical merits.

This strategy has yet to bear fruit. Some of management's initiatives seem to have worked—it is growing high-margin adjacencies, including payments via Venmo and debit card transactions. The company has promising partnerships with Perplexity and OpenAI for agentic commerce. But none of this has been enough to bend the curve—overall branded checkout volumes haven't accelerated, and in fact, look to be going the wrong way, decelerating in Q4. It cited slower progress on some other initiatives, as well as a weaker macro environment. But whatever the reason, the business continues to lose share.

Since we purchased the shares, consensus estimates for 2026 earnings are actually up a bit (5%). But the multiple has cratered—the stock now trades for about 9.8X 2026 earnings. We think that earnings per share could grow almost double digit in 2026. The current valuation seems to reflect the lack of growth acceleration. But the company is still growing, nicely profitable and cash generative. We remain cautious given the lack of evidence that management's plan is working, but we believe the current valuation limits further downside. At the same time, if management accelerates growth, we see significant upside to both the profitability and the share price. The only saving grace here is that we bought only a small position.

Given that PayPal has declined about 30% since our purchase while the market has gone up around 17% over the same time period, it is unlikely to ever be a good investment from our initial cost. But it looks like it could be a pretty good return from where we sit today.

data.title: Unite Group: Fears Overblown Amid Sound Strategy
data.description:
Introduction

Unite Group (UTGPF) is a UK-listed REIT that is the largest provider of Purpose Built Student Accommodation (PBSA) in the country. Its shares have declined by around 29% in the last 12 months, and I believe that this drop is overdone and now presents an opportunity for patient investors.

Why the Stock Has Dropped

In my opinion, the principal cause of this decline was news on 8 October 2025 that the final phase of the company’s sales cycle for the 2025/26 academic year had disappointed on volumes, with only 95.2% of beds sold, compared to 97.5% at the same point one year previously (although rental growth for rooms that were sold was close to expectations).

As it is, the shares had already been drifting downwards for some time, in my view primarily because of political uncertainty around tightening international student visa restrictions and broader signs of pressure on the overall tertiary higher education sector. Skepticism around a pending acquisition, that of peer company Empiric Student Property, is also likely to have contributed.

To understand why the shares have become attractive, I will first briefly recap the business model and market positioning before outlining the recent pressures and the logic behind the recent deal. Then I'll discuss why I believe the valuation is too low.

Why Unite is in a Strong Position

As the largest PBSA company in the UK, Unite has around 15% market share of corporate PBSA (and less than 5% of the entire non-university-owned accommodation market). Over 80% of its facilities serve ‘high-tariff’ universities (i.e., academically demanding and highly rated for research). Approximately one quarter of the group’s assets at 31 December 2024 were held in joint ventures with university partners, a strategy they expect to pursue further.

As a result of the late January 2026 closure of a cash and stock deal to acquire peer Empiric Student Property, the combined group has around 71,000 beds in 29 cities across the country. Around half of the company's rooms are sold into the student market via the ‘nominations’ process, whereby universities commit financially to block reservations in a property and invoice the students themselves. Such deals can be for a single year or multi-year contracts. In the latter case, rent rises tied to inflation (with a floor and cap, typically leading to increases of 3 to 4% per annum) are embedded into the deal. Nomination agreements are typically targeted at first-year students, in particular undergraduates.

Before the Empiric deal closing on 28 January 2026, the student and property profile was as follows (I will discuss the combined entity later):

The Environment Has Undoubtedly Softened but is Stabilising

After many years of expansion in the sector, the UK tertiary education industry has recently come under political and economic pressure.

Political pressure has grown because international student numbers have been large contributors to historically high immigration figures. In my view, this has become politically contentious in a context of rising cost of living and housing shortages. While the UK had a general election in July 2024 and a change of government, changes both before and since the election have tightened student visa eligibility, in particular the ability of students to bring dependents with them on their visas.

While lower-tariff universities are under particular pressure, as arguably these will have been among the most lax with regard to student quality and eligibility, Unite is overwhelmingly exposed to the best-positioned entities (with a heavy focus on Russell Group colleges, a number of which are in the top 100 such institutions in the world).

Furthermore, the UK is not unique in the English-speaking world with regard to rule changes for international students, with the US, Canada, and Australia also tightening student visa eligibility, improving the UK's relative position.

These rule changes have undoubtedly had an impact on overseas student numbers, but the situation appears to be stabilizing, as these slides from the November analyst day demonstrate:

However, it is true that a late-cycle disappointment in Chinese post-graduate student applications was a significant driver of the 2025/26 occupancy shortfall, with geopolitical tensions cited as the main driver.

There are also some domestic pressures becoming clear as the UK grapples with above-target inflation and a lackluster jobs market. Students in some tiers appear to be more sensitive to value-for-money considerations. However, this is manifesting far more in low-tariff and mid-tariff universities, where there has been a move towards commuting from home rather than the student housing experience:

One favorable factor worth highlighting is that over the next few years is a demographic one. The number of 18-year-olds in the UK, which is highly correlated to university demand, will grow by nearly 7% between 2025 and 2030:

New Supply Has Come at a Bad Time, but This Headwind Should Dissipate and is Concentrated in a Few Markets

Unfortunate timing has meant that an uptick in new supply hit the market around the time of student visa tightening, creating a ‘double whammy’ effect, which I believe to be temporary, as illustrated by this forecast:

Note that even this short-term increase in supply is at half the levels of the peak around 2017-2018. Construction and land costs, higher interest rates, and increased building safety regulations are such that the viability of new projects, especially for off-campus construction, is very limited at the moment. Hence the forecast from several sources for a decline in new supply in the next couple of years.

Unite’s management noted at their November 2025 analyst day that most of the weakness in their portfolio had been concentrated in a handful of markets, as this chart shows:

More than half of the shortfall in 2025/26 sales compared to 2024/25 was accounted for by the bottom three cities. Most of the rest was due to sales challenges from new builds and refurbs with unfortunate timing in the sales cycle, whereby potential occupants appear to have been put off by concerns about completion dates. This is unlikely to be a major recurring problem.

A Note on UK REIT Accounting and Analysis Metrics

Before analyzing the recent deal and current valuation metrics, for the benefit of those less familiar with this jurisdiction, it’s worth briefly reviewing some differences in UK REIT accounting and valuation practice compared to US GAAP and NAREIT-compliant practice in the United States.

Under the applicable IFRS rules, instead of depreciating property assets over time, UK REIT income statements include adjustments for changes in asset valuations and other non-cash adjustments. As a result, to aid comparability, under European Public Real Estate Association guidelines, the companies also report ‘EPRA earnings’ (which exclude movements relating to these changes) and EPRA Net Tangible Assets (NTA) valuations that are obtained regularly from external providers. These metrics, together with widely used cap rate comparisons, will help to contextualize valuations.

The Empiric Acquisition Makes Sense, and the Price is Attractive

In my opinion, the Empiric acquisition, first proposed in June 2025 and formally agreed upon in August 2025, is a sensible deal that broadens and strengthens the portfolio. This is because the customer profile is complementary to Unite’s core first-year undergraduate business built around a classic larger-scale ‘hall of residence’ model. Empiric properties are smaller and younger (average age 10 years versus 14 for legacy Unite) with more of a studio focus and allow for a more ‘independent living’ experience, with some buildings even incorporating apartments of 1 to 3 bedrooms.

As can be seen below, the typical Empiric customer is either a returning undergraduate or a postgraduate student:

Unite has identified an opportunity to capture more of the ‘returning student’ market by having more properties suitable for marketing to existing customers who are already committed to living in a given town for several years. This could be especially attractive given that a large proportion of returning students currently live in Houses of Multiple Occupation (‘HMOs’). These are essentially apartments configured to be suitable for groups of individuals as opposed to family units. This element of student accommodation provision is under sustained pressure from increased government regulation and taxation of the property owners.

Management has given their take on the Total Addressable Market as follows:

In my view, the terms are certainly attractive enough for Unite to justify the acquisition. The final terms were 0.085 shares of Unite Group (then trading at 731p) and 32p in cash. At close, owing to the fall in the Unite share price, the effective acquisition price was 80.6p per Empiric share, a 33% discount to its 30 June 2025 Net Tangible Assets. Empiric shareholders now own around 10% of the combined company. Loan-to-value (LTV) is expected to rise modestly from 26% to 29% as a result of the deal.

While the implied cap rate on day one NOI is around 6%, management expects this to be the foundation of an IRR from the deal in the low to mid-teens via the following levers:

An improvement to 6.5% from a recovery in occupancy from temporarily depressed levels (only 89% sold for 2025/26), an annual pricing escalation of 3% to 4%, and a benefit from the portion of the expected £14m annual synergies that falls into the NOI calculation (i.e., are direct operating costs of running the facilities). This, to me, is a credible outline of the path to more than acceptable returns.

They expect the deal to be accretive in the year to 31 December 2027 given the synergies. Furthermore, they are clearly expressing their faith in Empiric’s focus by planning to convert around 18 existing Unite properties to the Empiric model.

The Company’s Core Strategy Seems Sensible

Looking forward, Unite management has stated that they will be disciplined with regards to new construction, given the reduced financial viability of new projects, particularly off-campus ones. Their leading position across the UK’s best university towns should, in my view, help them identify and develop on-campus-focused relationships. (Indeed, in recent weeks the company has announced two new formal joint ventures with Newcastle University and Manchester Metropolitan University). They expect to keep leverage in the mid-twenties in the medium term, with surplus capital directed towards a stock buyback program, targeting £100m of repurchases through June 2026.

The Shares Are Already Baking a Lot of Pessimism

And now to why I believe the shares currently present an attractive entry point: there’s a lot in the price already.

On a trailing basis, Unite is trading at a cap rate of around 7%. I would argue that this is a depressed valuation given the predictability of its business, its leading platform, and the likelihood, in my view, that the company’s performance is likely to improve over the next 12 to 18 months as previously outlined.

For some additional context, while there are no true like-for-like comparable UK public companies, I think it’s helpful to compare the stock with the following:

Xior Student Housing (XIORF) is a publicly traded student accommodation pure play that trades in Brussels. With a market capitalization of €1.4bn and net debt of around €1.5bn, its trailing cap rate is a much lower 5.4%, despite LTV being substantially higher at nearly 50% (admittedly, lower-cost euro-denominated debt helps, and asset occupancy is at an excellent 98%).

With regard to cap rates for assets changing hands privately, information in this domain is, of course, more difficult to come by, but some recent estimates again imply that Unite is trading at a relatively low valuation. Commercial real estate firm Cushman & Wakefield's October 2025 report suggests cap rates for PBSA of 4.25% in Prime London and Regional varying from 5.25% to 6.5%, depending on quality. I believe this also supports my view that Unite is trading at an inappropriately low valuation given its preeminence in attractive markets.

In light of these comparisons, I would argue that a substantially lower cap rate would be justified, perhaps around 6%, which would imply a share price upside of around 24-25% to around 720p, compared to the current price of 578p.

Looked at differently, and using historic Tangible Book Value as a proxy for the regularly reported EPRA Net Tangible Assets, the shares trade at a historically depressed Price to Tangible Book Value of around 0.65x, with similar lows last touched in late 2011, when it’s worth noting additionally that the Loan-To-Value ratio was a much higher 54% compared to today’s 29%:

Given the stock has traded around TBV in the long term, but the current environment being a little challenging, some discount might be expected, say to 0.8-0.9x, in my view. Even the low end of this range would imply something over 710p, implying 23% upside.

The Dividend Seems Secure Given the Encouraging Forward-looking Signs

Based on total dividends paid in 2025 of 37.7p, the shares are currently yielding around 6.3%. Assuming the mid-point of current expected 2025 EPRA adjusted earnings of 47.9p (guidance range is 47.5-48.25p), this equates to dividend cover of around 1.27 times.

Risks

Stock-specific risks include management's execution of the marketing and pricing of their product, construction risk, and the maintenance of good relationships with partners. Industry risks include economic and political dislocation that reduces demand for student accommodation.

Investors should note that Unite Group’s principal listing is on the London Stock Exchange under the ticker UTG. In the United States, it can also be bought via a broker offering OTC trading under the ticker UTGPF. Liquidity in this latter market may be especially limited. As the company is based entirely in the UK and the equity and dividends are priced in GBP, investors outside the UK face currency risk. Non-UK investors in particular should seek advice on the tax treatment of dividends paid by a UK REIT.

Conclusion

Overall, while the Covid-era days of very strong pricing are certainly long gone, I believe Unite stock has been unreasonably penalized by the market for some short-term dislocations and excessive worries.

data.title: There Are Simply Better Options Than Fidelity Blue Chip Value
data.description:
After several years of below-average returns, value ETFs may finally be having their year in 2026. An article from February 17 that appeared on Seeking Alpha detailed support for value stocks from Jeremy Siegel, professor emeritus of finance at the University of Pennsylvania’s Wharton School. Siegel noted the surge in value stocks relative to their growth counterparts. This echoed other articles on this website that have said the timing may finally be right for large-cap value investing.

One way to play this hypothesis is the Fidelity Blue Chip Value ETF (FBCV). With a portfolio of stocks that trade at an average multiple well below that of the S&P 500, FBCV has gotten off to a good start this year with a gain of over 5% while the S&P 500 has booked a fractional loss over the last six and a half weeks. However, after looking at this Fidelity ETF in more detail, I think investors would be better served buying other options in the value space. My opinion of FBCV is a Hold, given the information provided below.

ETF Overview

Launched by Fidelity at the start of June 2020, the Fidelity Blue Chip Value ETF has the stated objective of long-term capital growth. It is actively managed and considered to be a semi-transparent ETF. Unlike most other ETFs, FBCV does not publicly release its holdings on a daily basis.

Fidelity does give some additional information about its selection criteria for the fund as part of its strategy on its Fact Sheet. It states that FBCV will invest at least 80% of its assets in “well-known, well-established, and well-capitalized” blue-chip companies. These companies will fall into either large-cap or mid-cap segments of the market. Fidelity’s selection method considers factors like assets, sales, earnings, or cash flow when adding or removing stocks from its portfolio. The fund uses the Russell 1000 Value Index to be its benchmark.

FBCV is a relatively small fund when measured by assets under management. At just $185.5 million, its size pales in comparison to many more traditional value funds like the iShares S&P 500 Value ETF (IVE), the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD), and the Vanguard Russell 1000 Value Index ETF (VONV), which all have at least $15 billion in AUM. The last time that Fidelity gave a full report of the fund’s portfolio, there were 113 holdings with just over 26% invested in the 10 largest that are listed below.

There is quite a lot of contrast between the top two names on this list. Energy giant Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) has gained almost 33% in the last 12 months, with the majority of those gains taking place since the start of the new year. Mag 7 member Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) has risen over 64% in the last 12 months but dropped about 12% since the start of February. In spite of its place in this value portfolio, Alphabet currently has an “F” in Valuation from Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating. Both Exxon and Alphabet have allocations just over 4% in FBCV. No other stock is over 3%, with the closest being Bank of America Corporation (BAC) at 2.87%.

The fund’s sector allocations are shown below alongside some other characteristics of the fund itself. When compared to its benchmark index, the fund is overweight Financials, Energy, and Consumer Staples. It is significantly underweight Information Technology and Real Estate. The biggest changes made by the fund in the final quarter of 2025 were to increase its exposure to Consumer Staples while decreasing its tech holdings. As part of its fund report from Q4 2025, Fidelity stated that Consumer Staples stocks were as cheap as they had ever been relative to the Russell index.

On the surface, the portfolio characteristics for FBCV look impressive, as it appears to have a lower valuation than its stated benchmark index while at the same time sporting a higher return on equity and earnings growth. Sales growth over the last year outpaced the Russell 1000 Value Index by about 60 basis points. Earnings per share growth over the last five years is higher by about 100 basis points. However, when we look at the performance of the fund, the returns do align with these metrics.

ETF Analysis

As those who have followed my previous articles on this site know, I am a big fan of value investing as a whole. As Fidelity states in its most recent quarterly review, the market value of a stock will eventually fall in line with its true worth over an extended period of time, and I agree with this assessment. The fund managers for FBCV state that their emphasis on metrics like return on invested capital, return on equity, and cash flow growth will help it differentiate quality value investments that do not ultimately turn into value traps. The characteristics of the fund listed above seem to substantiate this belief.

The results from FBCV, however, have been largely disappointing, at least on a comparative basis with other value-oriented funds. Morningstar gives FBCV only a three-star rating, saying the fund offers below-average risk along with below-average returns. The ETF has finished in the bottom quartile for all large-cap value funds in returns in two of the last three years. The fund's only year since its inception that it landed in the top quartile was 2022, a year when the fund produced losses of about 2.5%. In the first month and a half of 2026, a time when value investing is supposed to be becoming more popular, the fund is trailing its category in returns once again.

When compared to other peer value funds, the active management style followed by the Fidelity Blue Chip Value ETF does not appear to be paying off for its investors. Consider the price returns below from the other funds mentioned earlier, two of which are designed to follow the exact same benchmark as FBCV. The iShares S&P 500 Value ETF tops the price return from FBCV by a wide margin over the three- and five-year time spans. However, the two funds that follow the same benchmark as FBCV also come out on top over these time periods.

FBCV

The returns above do not include dividends, and FBCV does have a higher dividend yield than the others. The fund currently yields 2.79%, while the other three have a yield that is roughly 100 to 120 basis points lower. Increasing dividends do appear to be a strength for the fund, as its five-year dividend growth rate is over 58%.

However, even when dividend payouts are factored into the overall picture, all three funds above still come out on top over the five-year period as shown below. The added payouts from FBCV, even when reinvested, do not make up for the gap in price performance. For the funds that follow the Russell 1000 Value Index (VONV), they get the edge over the last five years, 70.5% vs. 67.8% for FBCV.

Admittedly, these differences are not enormous, but the final factor for me when evaluating FBCV is its considerably higher management fee. Fidelity charges fund holders 0.57% on an annual basis. This is above the median for all ETFs and over three times as large as the fees charged by these peer funds. The other funds listed in the comparison above have fees no greater than 0.18%. VONV has the lowest expense ratio at just six basis points.

Risks to Consider

There are two significant risks that come with an investment in FBCV. The first is the fund’s active management, which leaves the fund open to the possibility of significant underperformance over time when compared to its benchmark. As shown in the returns above, the fund has trailed its passively managed value peers, but the gaps could be even wider in the future. The lack of transparency also leaves investors with the added risk that market prices will deviate from the fund’s true net asset value by a wider margin than a traditional ETF.

There is certainly no guarantee that value investing will have the banner year that some are predicting in 2026. Led by continued investments in AI, growth stocks may storm ahead as short-term interest rates continue to decline, and the economy seems ever resilient to challenges caused by tariffs, slow job growth, and political uncertainty. If the US economy does falter later this year, the sinking tide may take all boats down with it, which could create losses for FBCV as well as most other value-oriented funds. The fund’s added weight in the financial sector could be a drag on returns if banks in the US face rising defaults, slow loan growth, and compressed margins.

The Bottom Line

Seeking Alpha gives the Fidelity Blue Chip Value ETF a Buy rating with high marks in Momentum, Dividends, and Risk. However, the Risk grade of “A-” appears to overlook the fund’s high turnover rate, which is over twice the median for all other ETFs. In my opinion, the positives for the fund do not outweigh the negatives, which do include FBCV’s high expenses.

I agree with the overall sentiment that value investing may have more merit this year than it has shown in the past. However, I am not convinced that FBCV is one of the best ways for investors to take advantage of the shifting market tailwinds. I consider the fund a Hold at this time.

data.title: Artisan Select Equity Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Activity
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Artisan Select Equity Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Portfolio Discussion

Our top-performing stocks this quarter were Samsung (SSNLF), Alphabet (GOOG) and Lam Research Corp (LRCX).

Samsung and Alphabet were the top two performing stocks during the quarter. They were also the top two performing stocks last quarter. It is no surprise that the shares of both companies are benefiting from their exposure to artificial intelligence (AI).

Samsung shares rose 39% during the quarter. This is after a 36% increase in Q3. The company is one of three major manufacturers of memory semiconductors, which are a key input for new AI data centers. A good portion of the hyperscalers' (i.e., Alphabet, Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT)) massive capex is spent on memory chips. This tidal wave of demand from cash-rich tech companies has led to strong semiconductor pricing, which, of course, benefits profits. Just this month, Samsung announced its operating profits for Q4 had tripled from the prior year, and Samsung is poised for record results in 2026.

The semiconductor business is notoriously cyclical. In the past, the boom times led to overcapacity and periods of poor performance. There are compelling arguments for why this upcycle might be bigger and more durable than the past. Samsung is well positioned to capture its fair share of the market. The current fundamentals are excellent, and the company's outlook is very strong. We are watching the cycle and Samsung's valuation carefully, and we believe its shares are currently trading at a reasonable 9X earnings and below 2X book.

Alphabet's shares rose 29% during Q4 after a 38% rise in Q3. It's worth noting that the shares have more than doubled over the past eight months, adding over $2.2 trillion in market cap. In case regular readers of our quarterly letters were wondering, this is equivalent to over seven Coca-Cola Companies.

Similar to Samsung, AI is the primary driver of Alphabet's share price performance. After getting caught a little flat-footed by OpenAI's ChatGPT launch in 2022, Alphabet has reclaimed AI leadership. The company is differentiated within the AI industry due to its "full-stack" approach, spanning research/models, infrastructure and products. It is performing well across the entire stack. Its new Gemini 3 model and other products, such as Nano Banana, have been widely applauded and have gained solid traction with users. Its cloud business is performing well and has been able to leverage the company's AI strength to accelerate sales and profits. Alphabet is aggressively integrating AI tools and features across products and its massive user base. Most importantly, although for some reason less frequently noted, the core search business also continues to grow. In fact, search revenue growth actually accelerated in Q3.

The strong business performance is reflected in the share price. Alphabet's shares now trade at close to 30X earnings. This is the highest P/E since 2008. To put it another way, investors haven't been this optimistic since the market cap was less than $100 billion.

Revenues and profits at Alphabet are up about 20X versus 2008. While we are unlikely to see that type of performance over the next 17 years, we do expect the business to continue performing well. The core business remains very healthy. The company is exceptionally well positioned to maintain leadership in AI, given its combination of technology, infrastructure, distribution and a balance sheet to support the massive investments required to build out its AI capabilities. The regulatory and anti-trust issues are largely behind it, and management has demonstrated reasonable cost control and attention to profitability.

The market vastly undervalued Alphabet's business in 2008. From where we sit today, the valuation is neither cheap nor extreme, and the fundamentals appear strong for the foreseeable future. We are comfortable with our investment.

Lam's stock appreciated 28% in Q4 due to a strong calendar Q3 earnings report and increasing enthusiasm about the company's growth from AI investment.

The earnings report provided strong evidence of share gains, with revenue up 28% Y/Y, compared with market growth of 5%–10%. Lam's share gains have been driven by its leading exposure to the NAND market (which is finally recovering after years of underinvestment), new tools gaining share of incremental customer process steps and increasing deposition and etch intensity in leading-edge wafer fabrication. Each of these variables seems likely to drive faster-than-industry growth into 2026 and beyond.

In addition to the positive near-term financial performance, Lam's management provided a quantitative estimate of the industry's long-term exposure to AI—$8 billion of additional industry revenue for every $100 billion of data center investment. We believe this formal estimate has made it easier for investors to project future industry and Lam revenue growth based on their outlook for AI investment.

Our investment in Lam is becoming increasingly difficult to manage, given the run-up in share price and its elevated valuation based on historic earnings power. If we are in the early stages of the AI revolution, Lam will generate enormous cash flow over the next decade, possibly more than we could reasonably estimate. That said, we believe investors (and people in general) tend to both underestimate the long-term impact of large secular changes and overestimate the near-term impact. We don't know if AI is currently in the under- or the over-estimation phase, hence our equivocation.

Our worst performers were Meta (META), Progressive (PGR) and Universal Music (UMGNF).

Meta shares declined 10% during the quarter but rose 13% for the year. The business's financial and operational performance remains exceptional. The business saw accelerated growth in users and revenues. Daily active users on its platforms now exceed 3.5 billion, and time spent on Facebook and Instagram grew by double digits. The business grew revenues over 25% in Q3. Given the massive scale of the business, this level of growth is truly remarkable. The reason for the share price decline is a massive ramp-up in expenses. Operating expenses also grew 40% during Q3, and Meta signaled even more investment in the future. The investments, of course, are related to its AI initiatives.

We share the market's concern around the quantum of investment. More specifically, we were concerned that the company might abandon its commitment to growing consolidated operating profits. After conversations with the company, we are more comfortable that it understands the importance of upholding its commitments and maintaining investor credibility. The investments in AI infrastructure are clearly going to be massive, but we are hopeful the company will find a reasonable balance between its investments while maintaining financial discipline and profit growth.

Meta shares are trading at 21X to 22X P/E, which is not demanding given its strong fundamental performance, especially considering the significant investment that is already running through the income statement to support its Reality Labs and AI investments.

Progressive shares declined 8% during the quarter. For much of the past two years, Progressive has benefited from a favorable competitive position and grown much faster than the market. The business is still performing well, but the excess growth and underwriting profitability is now normalizing from a high level. This was expected and included in our valuation methodology—which prices the shares on normalized profit levels. The shares are currently trading at 13X–14X our estimate of normalized earnings, which is an attractive price for an advantaged franchise business like Progressive.

Universal Music Group was down 9% for the quarter. There is nothing much to report here. The business continues to chug along. Q3 revenue was up 10% at constant currency and 5% on a reported basis. EBITDA (the only profit measure it reports in its abbreviated Q3 release) was up 12% at constant currency or 7% on an actual basis. We continue to believe the business has a long runway for revenue growth driven by a combination of pricing power and subscriber growth, as well as margin expansion potential.

Earnings power is a figure that telegraphs a business's ability to generate profits over the long term, assuming all current operational conditions generally remain constant. Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation and Amortization (EBITDA) is an indicator of a company's financial performance which is calculated by looking at earnings before the deduction of interest expenses, taxes, depreciation and amortization.
